learningGoal: A brilliant shortcut: when letters are missing, try the vowels (A, E, I, O, U) first!
Why vowels are often the missing piece and how testing them cracks the word in seconds

screens.title: What vowels are hiding in "B _ K E"?
screens.body:
Daisy is working out a word with missing vowels.

**Did you know?** Every English word needs at least one vowel (A, E, I, O, U) — so when you see blanks, try the vowels first! It works more often than you'd think.

**B _ K E** — which vowel completes this word?

screens.title: Vowels first — give it a go!
screens.body: Look at **B _ K E** — which letters are missing? When letters are missing from a word, try slotting in vowels (A, E, I, O, U) — they're the most commonly missing letters.

interaction.sentence: When letters are missing from a word, try slotting in ____ first — they're the most commonly missing letters.
interaction.correctFeedback: That's right — vowels (A, E, I, O, U) are the most commonly missing letters! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: The answer is "vowels". A, E, I, O, U are the five vowels — and they're the most commonly missing letters. ✓

steps.text: Look at the consonants (all the letters that aren't vowels) you CAN see: B _ K E
steps.why: The consonant skeleton gives you the word's shape

steps.text: Try vowels in the gaps: A, E, I, O, U
steps.why: Which vowel(s) make a real word?

steps.text: Answer: A → BAKE
steps.why: to cook in an oven ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letter(s) complete **C _ K E**?

interaction.question: Which letter(s) complete C _ K E?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! C + O + KE = COKE — a type of fizzy drink. The missing vowel is O. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "O". C + O + KE = COKE — a type of fizzy drink. The missing vowel is O. ✓

screens.title: Vowels first — you've got the trick!
screens.body: Nice one! Here's your go-to method when letters are missing:

steps.text: 1. Look at the consonants you can see
steps.why: They give you the word's skeleton

steps.text: 2. Try A, E, I, O, U in the gaps
steps.why: Vowels are the most commonly missing letters

steps.text: 3. Say the word aloud — does it sound right?
steps.why: Your ears know more words than you think! ✓

learningGoal: Levelling up: spotting missing vowels in trickier words where two options look possible
How to choose the right vowel when you're torn between two — a real exam skill!

screens.title: Two vowels are hiding in "S _ L _ D"
screens.body:
Evie is double-checking her VR answers.

This word has **two vowels** missing — a bit trickier! But look at the consonants that are still there. They give you the **skeleton** of the word, like a puzzle outline.

**S _ L _ D** — can you work out which vowels fit?

screens.title: Read the consonant skeleton!
screens.body: Look at **S _ L _ D**. The consonants you can see are like a skeleton — they show you the word's shape. Now try different vowels (A, E, I, O, U) until you find a real word.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Read the skeleton, try vowels, say it aloud, then pick the real word. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: The correct order is: read the consonants, try vowel pairs, say it aloud, then pick the real word. ✓

steps.text: Skeleton: S _ L _ D
steps.why: These consonants narrow it down

steps.text: Try vowel pairs: AA?
steps.why: Does this make a word you know?

steps.text: SALAD — a cold dish of mixed vegetables
steps.why: Say it aloud to check ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which vowels complete **P _ N _ L**?

interaction.question: Which vowels complete P _ N _ L?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! P + A + N + E + L = PANEL — a flat section forming part of a surface. The vowels A and E fill the blanks. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "AE". P + A + N + E + L = PANEL — a flat section forming part of a surface. The vowels A and E fill the blanks. ✓

screens.title: The consonant skeleton — clever!
screens.body: Great work! When multiple vowels are missing, the consonants are your guide:

steps.text: 1. Read the consonants — they're the skeleton
steps.why: S_L_D → S?L?D — what fits?

steps.text: 2. Try common vowel pairs: AI, OE, IO, OU
steps.why: Many English words use these combinations

steps.text: 3. Say the whole word aloud to check
steps.why: SALAD, LEMON, ROBOT — trust your ears ✓

learningGoal: Flipping it around: sometimes the missing letters are consonants, not vowels!
A clever trick — looking at the vowels you CAN see helps you work out what's missing

screens.title: Which consonants complete "_ A _ E"?
screens.body:
Daisy is working out which consonants are missing.

Here's a twist — the **vowels are still there** but the **consonants** are missing! Use the vowels as your guide. Try saying the word out loud — the vowels tell you what it sounds like!

**_ A _ E** — which consonants fit?

screens.title: Vowels are your guide — listen to them!
screens.body: Look at **_ A _ E**. When consonants (letters like B, C, D, F, G...) are missing, the vowels you CAN see tell you what the word sounds like. Sound it out!

steps.text: Vowels visible: _ A _ E
steps.why: These tell you what the word sounds like

steps.text: Sound it out: what word has these vowels in this pattern?
steps.why: Think of words you know that sound like this

steps.text: Answer: G T → GATE
steps.why: a hinged barrier used to close an opening in a wall or fence ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which consonant(s) complete **_ A _ E**?

interaction.question: Which consonant(s) complete _ A _ E?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! L + A + K + E = LAKE — a large body of water surrounded by land. The vowels A and E were your guide; L and K completed the word. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "L K". L + A + K + E = LAKE — a large body of water surrounded by land. The vowels A and E were your guide; L and K completed the word. ✓

screens.title: Missing consonants — sorted!
screens.body: Brilliant! Now you can handle missing consonants too. Here's the method:

steps.text: 1. Read the vowels — they show the sound pattern
steps.why: _A_E = something that sounds like 'aye'

steps.text: 2. Think of words with that vowel pattern
steps.why: GATE, BAKE, LAKE, CAKE — lots of options!

steps.text: 3. Check which consonants match the options given
steps.why: Only one set of consonants will be in the list ✓

learningGoal: Double letters are a massive clue! How to spot when TT, LL, SS or similar are hiding
Common words with double letters that pop up all the time in the exam

screens.title: Spot the double letters in "B _ TT _ R"!
screens.body:
Daisy is solving a word with double letters hiding.

**Did you know?** Double letters like TT, DD, PP or BB appear in HUNDREDS of English words. Spotting a double is like finding a big neon clue — it narrows things down fast!

**B _ TT _ R** — what goes in the gaps?

screens.title: Double letters = massive clue!
screens.body: Look at **B _ TT _ R**. When you see double letters (TT, DD, PP, BB, LL, SS), they narrow down the word hugely. Think of words with that double-letter pattern.

steps.text: Spot the doubles: B _ TT _ R
steps.why: Double letters are unusual — they limit the options

steps.text: Think of words with this double pattern
steps.why: How many words do you know with these doubles?

steps.text: Answer: U E → BUTTER
steps.why: a soft yellow food made from cream ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letters complete **K _ TT _ N**?

interaction.question: Which letters complete K _ TT _ N?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! K + I + TT + E + N = KITTEN — a young cat. The double T was visible, and I and E filled the gaps. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "I E". K + I + TT + E + N = KITTEN — a young cat. The double T was visible, and I and E filled the gaps. ✓

screens.title: Double-letter detective — case closed!
screens.body: Well done! You're a double-letter detective now. Here's what to remember:

steps.text: 1. Notice the doubles: TT, DD, PP, BB, LL, SS
steps.why: These narrow down the word massively

steps.text: 2. Think of words you know with those doubles
steps.why: BUTTER, LADDER, RABBIT, HAPPEN, KITTEN...

steps.text: 3. Check which one fits the other visible letters
steps.why: The surrounding letters confirm your guess ✓

learningGoal: How to crack words with missing letters at the start — read the ending first!
Why the end of a word is often your biggest clue to what's at the beginning

screens.title: What starts "_ _ AIN"?
screens.body:
Ella is working out a word where the start is missing.

When the **beginning** of a word is missing, here's a top tip: read the **ending** first! The ending often tells you what kind of word it is, and that helps you guess the start.

**_ _ AIN** — what letters go at the start?

screens.title: Read the ending — it's your biggest clue!
screens.body: Look at **_ _ AIN** — the start is missing! When the start of a word is missing, the ending is your best clue. Read the ending, then think of words that end that way.

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Read the ending, brainstorm words, check the options, then pick the real word. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: The correct order is: read the ending, think of matching words, check the options, then pick the real word. ✓

steps.text: Read the ending: _ _ AIN
steps.why: What does this word END with?

steps.text: Think of words ending this way
steps.why: How many words end with these letters?

steps.text: Answer: BR → BRAIN
steps.why: the organ inside your head that controls your body ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letters go at the **start** of **_ _ AMP**?

interaction.question: Which letters complete _ _ AMP?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! ST + AMP = STAMP — a small sticky label for posting letters. The ending AMP was visible; ST completed the start. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "ST". ST + AMP = STAMP — a small sticky label for posting letters. The ending AMP was visible; ST completed the start. ✓

screens.title: Start-gap strategy — nailed it!
screens.body: Great work! When the beginning of a word is missing, you now know what to do:

steps.text: 1. Read the ENDING of the word first
steps.why: The ending narrows down your options hugely

steps.text: 2. Think of words that end this way
steps.why: _AIN → BRAIN, CHAIN, DRAIN, TRAIN...

steps.text: 3. Check which starting letters are in the options
steps.why: Only one option will make a real word ✓

learningGoal: How to solve words with missing letters at the end — common endings are your secret weapon!
Why knowing endings like ING, TION, LY and ED gives you a massive head start

screens.title: What finishes "FORE _ _ _"?
screens.body:
Daisy is completing a word where the ending is missing.

When the **ending** of a word is missing, the **start** is your best friend! Read the beginning and then think about common word endings — ING, TION, LY, ED. One of them will click!

**FORE _ _ _** — what letters finish this word?

screens.title: Read the start — then think endings!
screens.body: Look at **FORE _ _ _** — the ending is missing! Read the start and think of common English endings: -DOM, -FUL, -MENT, -NESS, -ING, -TION.

interaction.correctFeedback: Well matched! KINGDOM, HELPFUL, EXCITEMENT, KINDNESS — common endings make word-guessing much easier. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: The correct pairs are: KINGDOM, HELPFUL, EXCITEMENT, KINDNESS. Learning common endings like -DOM, -FUL, -MENT and -NESS helps you solve end-gap puzzles fast. ✓

steps.text: Read the start: FORE _ _ _
steps.why: What word begins with these letters?

steps.text: Try common endings: DOM, FUL, ING, MENT, NESS
steps.why: English has lots of standard word endings

steps.text: Answer: VER → FOREVER
steps.why: for all time, without end ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letters go at the **end** of **WONDER _ _ _**?

interaction.question: Which letters complete WONDER _ _ _?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! WONDER + FUL = WONDERFUL — extremely good or marvellous. The suffix -FUL means 'full of'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "FUL". WONDER + FUL = WONDERFUL — extremely good or marvellous. The suffix -FUL means 'full of'. ✓

screens.title: End-gap strategy — sorted!
screens.body: Brilliant! When the ending of a word is missing, common endings are your shortcut:

steps.text: 1. Read the START of the word first
steps.why: It tells you what the word is about

steps.text: 2. Try common English endings
steps.why: -DOM, -FUL, -MENT, -NESS, -ING, -TION

steps.text: 3. Pick the ending that makes a real word
steps.why: KINGDOM, HELPFUL, FREEDOM — trust what sounds right ✓

learningGoal: The sandwich method: use the start AND end of a word to work out what's in the middle!
How knowing the outside letters helps you fill in the gap — like a word sandwich

screens.title: What's hiding in the middle of "CH _ _ SE"?
screens.body:
Daisy is working out the middle of a word.

When letters are missing from the **middle**, you've actually got an advantage — the **start AND the end** are both visible! Think of them like bread in a sandwich, and the missing letters are the filling.

**CH _ _ SE** — what filling goes in the middle?

screens.title: The sandwich method — bread first!
screens.body: Look at **CH _ _ SE** — the middle is missing! Read the start of the word, then the end. Think of words that begin and end this way — the answer must fit in the middle like a sandwich!

steps.text: Start: read the first letters of CH _ _ SE
steps.why: What does the word begin with?

steps.text: End: read the last letters
steps.why: What does the word end with?

steps.text: Sandwich: EE → CHEESE
steps.why: a food made from milk curds ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letters go in the **middle** of **FR _ _ ZE**?

interaction.question: Which letters complete FR _ _ ZE?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! FR + EE + ZE = FREEZE — to turn into ice or become very cold. The start FR and end ZE sandwiched the answer EE. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "EE". FR + EE + ZE = FREEZE — to turn into ice or become very cold. The start FR and end ZE sandwiched the answer EE. ✓

screens.title: The sandwich method — delicious!
screens.body: You've got the sandwich method down! When letters are missing from the middle:

steps.text: 1. Read the START of the word
steps.why: This is one slice of bread

steps.text: 2. Read the END of the word
steps.why: This is the other slice

steps.text: 3. What fits in the middle?
steps.why: CH__SE → CHEESE, BR__GE → BRIDGE ✓

learningGoal: Levelling up: tackling trickier middle-gap words with confidence
How to use the sandwich method at speed so you can fly through these in the exam

screens.title: Can you fill the sandwich? "WH _ _ PER"
screens.body:
Evie is practising more middle-gap puzzles.

You already know the sandwich method — brilliant! Let's level up with some trickier words. The start and end are visible, middle is missing.

**WH _ _ PER** — what's the filling?

screens.title: Sandwich method — next level!
screens.body: Look at **WH _ _ PER**. For trickier words, try sounding out each option with the start and end letters — one will click!

steps.text: Start + End: WH _ _ PER
steps.why: Your sandwich bread

steps.text: Try each option in the gap
steps.why: Sound them out: which makes a real word?

steps.text: IS → WHISPER
steps.why: to speak very softly ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which letters complete **SL _ _ PER**?

interaction.question: Which letters go in the middle of SL _ _ PER?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! SL + IP + PER = SLIPPER — a soft shoe worn indoors. The start SL and end PER sandwiched IP perfectly. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "IP". SL + IP + PER = SLIPPER — a soft shoe worn indoors. The start SL and end PER sandwiched IP perfectly. ✓

screens.title: Sandwich master — level up!
screens.body: You're smashing it! The sandwich method works on all sorts of words:

steps.text: Read START + END = your two clues
steps.why: They narrow down the word massively

steps.text: Sound out each option in the gap
steps.why: One will click — trust your ears

steps.text: WHISPER, SHELTER, THUNDER, WINDOW
steps.why: The sandwich method works every time ✓

learningGoal: Pattern power! How recognising combos like TH, SH, CH and CK makes you faster
Why knowing these patterns is like having a cheat code for missing-letter questions

screens.title: Spot the pattern in "_ _ APTER"!
screens.body:
Daisy is spotting a common letter pattern in a word.

**Did you know?** English has common **letter pairs** that pop up again and again: CH, SH, TH, CK, GH, SK, PH, WH. Once you learn them, they're like having a cheat code for these questions!

**_ _ APTER** — which common pattern fits?

screens.title: Spot the pattern — it's your superpower!
screens.body: Look at **_ _ APTER**. English has standard letter combinations (like CH, SH, TH) that appear in thousands of words. If you know them, you can solve missing-letter questions much faster.

steps.text: Common patterns: CH, SH, TH, CK, GH, SK, PH, WH
steps.why: These appear in hundreds of words

steps.text: Look at _ _ APTER — which pattern fits?
steps.why: Try each one until you find a real word

steps.text: Answer: CH → CHAPTER
steps.why: a section of a book ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which common letter pattern completes **_ _ URCH**?

interaction.question: Which letter pattern completes _ _ URCH?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! CH + URCH = CHURCH — a building used for Christian worship. The CH pattern appears at both the start and end of this word! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "CH". CH + URCH = CHURCH — a building used for Christian worship. The CH pattern appears at both the start and end of this word! ✓

screens.title: Pattern power — unlocked!
screens.body: Excellent! These common letter patterns will speed you up massively:

steps.text: CH (chapter, chicken, church, cheese)
steps.why: One of the most common patterns

steps.text: SH, TH, WH, PH (ship, think, whale, phone)
steps.why: H-combinations appear everywhere

steps.text: CK, SK, GH, NK (back, basket, knight, think)
steps.why: Consonant clusters to watch for ✓

learningGoal: Your safety net: how to use elimination when you're stuck on a tricky one
Why crossing out impossible options is way smarter (and faster!) than random guessing

screens.title: Stuck? Try elimination! "_ _ _ NITURE"
screens.body:
Daisy is stuck on a tricky missing-letter question.

Feeling stuck? Don't worry — there's always a way through! **Try each option** and cross out the ones that don't make a real word. This is called **process of elimination**, and it's your safety net when nothing else clicks.

**_ _ _ NITURE** — which option makes a real word?

screens.title: Cross out the fakes — be ruthless!
screens.body: Look at **_ _ _ NITURE**. When you're stuck, try each option one by one. If it doesn't make a real word, cross it out — the last one standing is your answer!

interaction.sentence: When you're stuck, try each option and ____ the ones that don't make real words.
interaction.correctFeedback: Exactly! Cross out (eliminate) the fakes — the last one standing is your answer. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: The answer is "cross out". Try each option, cross out the fakes, and the last one standing is your answer. ✓

steps.text: Try each option with _ _ _ NITURE
steps.why: Plug in each set of letters

steps.text: Cross out any that don't make real words
steps.why: If you've never heard of it, it's probably not a word

steps.text: Answer: FUR → FURNITURE
steps.why: tables, chairs, beds and other items in a home ✓

screens.title: Your turn — eliminate!
screens.body: Try each option with **_ _ _ ATHLON** and cross out the fakes. Which one makes a real word?

interaction.question: Which letters complete _ _ _ ATHLON?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! DEC + ATHLON = DECATHLON — a sporting event with ten activities. TRIATHLON is a real word but with only three events, and PENATHLON, HEXATHLON and BIGATHLON don't exist. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "DEC". DEC + ATHLON = DECATHLON — a sporting event with ten activities. TRIATHLON is a real word but with only three events, and PENATHLON, HEXATHLON and BIGATHLON don't exist. ✓

screens.title: Elimination — you'll never be stuck!
screens.body: Now you've got a safety net for the trickiest questions. When nothing else works:

steps.text: 1. Try each option in the gap
steps.why: Plug them in one by one

steps.text: 2. Cross out any that don't make real words
steps.why: GARNITURE? SIGNITURE? Not real — cross them out!

steps.text: 3. The last one standing is your answer
steps.why: FURNITURE — the only real word left ✓

learningGoal: Taking elimination to the next level with harder words — you're ready for this!
How to build real speed by testing options in a smart, systematic way

screens.title: Eliminate to win! "_ _ _ GUAGE"
screens.body:
Evie is using elimination on a really tricky word.

These are harder words — but you've got this! The elimination method works every time. Try each option, cross out the fakes, and watch the answer reveal itself.

**_ _ _ GUAGE**

Options: **LAN**  |  **BAG**  |  **MOR**  |  **SAU**  |  **VER**

screens.title: Test smart, not random!
screens.body:
Look at **_ _ _ GUAGE**. Go through each option methodically — don't just stare at the word. Actively try each one!

Options: **LAN**  |  **BAG**  |  **MOR**  |  **SAU**  |  **VER**

steps.text: Option by option: try each with _ _ _ GUAGE
steps.why: Be systematic — don't skip any

steps.text: Most options will create nonsense words
steps.why: Cross those out straight away

steps.text: LAN → LANGUAGE
steps.why: the method of human communication using words ✓

screens.title: Eliminate!
screens.body: Which option completes **_ _ _ GRAPH**? Try each one!

interaction.question: Which letters complete _ _ _ GRAPH?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! PARA + GRAPH = PARAGRAPH — a section of writing starting on a new line. AUTOGRAPH is a real word but means a signature. Only PARAGRAPH matches! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "PARA". PARA + GRAPH = PARAGRAPH — a section of writing starting on a new line. AUTOGRAPH is a real word but means a signature. Only PARAGRAPH matches! ✓

screens.title: Elimination master — nothing can stop you!
screens.body: You've built up a brilliant toolkit! For even the hardest missing-letter questions:

steps.text: 1. Don't panic — even hard words follow the rules
steps.why: Every answer makes a real English word

steps.text: 2. Try EVERY option, not just the first one
steps.why: Be systematic — test them all

steps.text: 3. The fake words are usually obvious
steps.why: BAGGUAGE? SAUGUAGE? Cross them out and move on ✓
